{{Chess Opening Theory/Position |English attack |parent=[[../|Najdorf Sicilian]] }} == 6. Be3 · English attack == 6. Be3 is the '''English attack'''. White has an idea to castle long and storm Black's kingside with their pawns. [[/6...e5|'''6...e5''']] is the mainline. This drives off White's knight to b3, but weakens d5 which White will try to use as an outpost. [[/6...Ng4|'''6...Ng4''']] is the '''Anti-English'''. Black tries to avert White's plans of a kingside attack by trading off White's important dark square bishop. Whereas in the Yugoslav attack against the Dragon Sicilian, [[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...c5/2. Nf3/2...d6/3. d4/3...cxd4/4. Nxd4/4...Nf6/5. Nc3/5...g6/6. Be3/6...Ng4|6. Be3 Ng4?? is a mistake]] on account of 7. Bb5+! Nc6 8. Nxc6 bxc6 9. Bxc6+ Bd7 10. Bxa8, the difference in the Najdorf is that Black has played ...a6, so White does not have the resource Bb5+, therefore ...Ng4 is safe. [[/6...e6|'''6...e6''']] builds a "small centre" and transposes into a '''Scheveningen Scillian'''. This is move order ju-jitsu to play the Scheveningen without facing the Keres attack, 5...e6 6. g4!. In that line, Black cannot play 6...e5 on account of the Bb5+ resource. However, in the move order 5...a6 6. Be3 e6, Black's a6 pawn prevents Bb5+, so 7. g4!? can be met with ...e5!, a discovered attack on g4 and threatening ...exd4 with a fork. === History === 6. Be3 is the most popular way for White to combat the Najdorf. It has been played since the 1950s[https://www.chessgames.com/perl/chessgame?gid=1994034 Cardoso v Jezek, 1956. Chessgames.com], but 6. Bg5 was more typical until the 1980s, when 6. Be3 was popularised by English grandmasters like [[wikipedia:Tony Miles|Tony Miles]], [[wikipedia:John Nunn|John Nunn]] and [[wikipedia:Nigel Short|Nigel Short]].e.g. [https://www.chessgames.com/perl/chessgame?gid=1114091 Short v Portish, 1985 (Candidates). Chessgames.com] The approach--long castle and pawn storm--had been the standard approach against the Dragon Sicilian, [[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...c5/2. Nf3/2...d6/3. d4/3...cxd4/4. Nxd4/4...Nf6/5. Nc3/5...g6/6. Be3/6...Bg7/7. f3|the Yugoslav attack]], since at least the 1930s.e.g. [https://www.chessgames.com/perl/chessgame?gid=1090706 Levenfish vs Kan, 1939. Chessgames.com] ==Theory table== {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}}. 1.e4 c5 2.Nf3 d6 3.d4 cxd4 4.Nxd4 Nf6 5. Nc3 a6 6.Be3
